key_themes: Consumer protection and scam enforcement (e.g., student loan debt relief schemes, misleading earnings claims)
Restoring competition in digital advertising ecosystem (settlements with major ad agencies)
Antitrust, merger oversight, HSR threshold updates, and business collaboration guidance
Noncompete agreements, worker protections, and negative option/subscription rules
Shift in regulatory approach under new Chairman Andrew Ferguson (federal court preference, focus on fraud/content moderation)
summary: As of mid-April 2026, the FTC remains highly active with routine enforcement on consumer scams, deceptive marketing, and competition issues, including stopping alleged student loan debt relief fraud, a $1.5M settlement with Publishing.com, actions against noncompetes, and settlements with ad companies (WPP, Dentsu, Publicis) over digital advertising practices.[[1]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events)[[2]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases)[[3]](https://www.ftc.gov/about-ftc/bureaus-offices/press-releases) Updated HSR thresholds, joint DOJ guidance on collaborations, and a strategic plan emphasizing vigorous but prudent antitrust/consumer protection reflect continuity with some procedural shifts under Chairman Andrew Ferguson (e.g., preferring federal courts for mergers).[[4]](https://www.gtlaw.com/en/insights/2026/4/competition-currents-april-2026)[[5]](https://www.jw.com/news/hart-scott-rodino-2026/) Investor and social media discussion is relatively sparse with mixed views—positive on scam prevention and competition restoration, but criticism in niches like PBMs or potential crypto payment processor impacts. Overall financial sentiment is neutral: supportive of market integrity without major disruptive new rules roiling broad sectors like tech or M&A.[[6]](https://www.wsgr.com/en/insights/2026-year-in-preview-regulatory-consumer-protection-trends-for-companies-to-watch-out-for.html)
sources: 15+ FTC press releases and news articles (ftc.gov, CBS, NYT, legal updates from 2026), ~25 X posts reviewed (low engagement; semantic/keyword searches on regulatory impact, antitrust, enforcement)
